title: Maundy Thursday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## April 14, 2022 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

April 14, 2022 was Maundy Thursday, the day before Good Friday in Holy Week, occurring during the season of Lent. The RCL readings for Maundy Thursday typically include Exodus 12:1-14 (the Passover institution), Psalm 113, 1 Corinthians 11:23-26 (Paul's account of the Last Supper), and John 13:1-17, 31b-35 (Jesus washing the disciples' feet and the new commandment to love one another). This is one of the most solemn and theologically dense days in the Christian calendar, centered on Jesus's final meal with his disciples and his act of humble service.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of significant tension regarding sexuality and inclusion, with ongoing debates about same-sex marriage and LGBTQ+ clergy that had intensified since the 2018 General Convention. The Diocese of Colorado, which oversees Saint John's Cathedral, was led by Bishop Kimberly Lucas (elected 2021), who had signaled more progressive stances on these issues. No major General Convention actions or bishop elections were occurring in April 2022, but the broader denominational culture wars continued to simmer in parishes nationwide.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing unseasonably warm spring weather in mid-April 2022, with temperatures in the 70s-80s Fahrenheit. The Colorado legislature was in session (adjourned May 11, 2022), with debates ongoing about inflation, education funding, and various social bills. Downtown Denver and the Cathedral Hill neighborhood were continuing recovery post-pandemic, with restaurants and cultural institutions reopening to fuller capacity. No major crisis or weather event affected the immediate Denver area in the week before April 14.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the ongoing war in Ukraine, now six weeks in, with reports of Russian atrocities in Bucha generating international outcry and calls for stronger U.S. support. The Biden administration was navigating inflation and gas prices (reaching record highs by mid-month), becoming a central campaign issue ahead of the 2022 midterm elections in November. The Supreme Court had recently heard oral arguments in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (the case that would overturn Roe v. Wade), with a decision expected in June, creating significant national anxiety. The April 10 shooting in Brooklyn subway had occurred days prior, heightening fears about urban crime and gun violence. Johnny Depp's defamation trial against Amber Heard was in full swing and consuming significant media attention.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Russian invasion of Ukraine dominated global headlines, with reports emerging of mass graves in Bucha and other towns, escalating international condemnation and calls for war crimes investigations. NATO was responding with increased military aid to Ukraine and stronger rhetoric about supporting Kyiv through a prolonged conflict. China was implementing strict COVID-19 lockdowns in Shanghai and other major cities, disrupting global supply chains and drawing international criticism over the human cost of zero-COVID policy.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The United States in April 2022 was experiencing a pronounced moment of anxiety and division. The war in Ukraine had temporarily united much of the country in support for Ukraine, but domestic polarization remained severe over abortion (with the Dobbs decision looming), election integrity, and LGBTQ+ rights. There was widespread concern about inflation eroding purchasing power and recession risks. Culturally, entertainment remained a significant escape valve, with the Depp-Heard trial consuming tabloid and social media attention. The Denver Nuggets were in the midst of a strong 2021-22 NBA season (they would make the playoffs), offering local sports optimism. The broader American mood was one of precarity—geopolitical uncertainty abroad and deep internal divisions at home.
